title: A Study in Scarlet — Chapter 1 (excerpt)
content: In the year 1878 I took my degree of Doctor of Medicine of the University of London, and proceeded to Netley to go through the course prescribed for surgeons in the army. Having completed my studies there, I was duly attached to the Fifth Northumberland Fusiliers as Assistant Surgeon. The regiment was stationed in India at the time, and before I could join it, the second Afghan war had broken out. On landing at Bombay, I learned that my corps had advanced through the passes, and was already deep in the enemy's country. I followed, however, with many other officers who were in the same situation as myself, and succeeded in reaching Candahar in safety, where I found my regiment, and at once entered upon my new duties. The campaign brought honours and promotion to many, but for me it had nothing but misfortune and disaster. I was removed from my brigade and attached to the Berkshires, with whom I served at the fatal battle of Maiwand. There I was struck on the shoulder by a Jezail bullet, which shattered the bone and grazed the subclavian artery. I should have fallen into the hands of the murderous Ghazis had it not been for the devotion and courage shown by Murray, my orderly, who threw me across a pack-horse, and succeeded in bringing me safely to the British lines. My name is John H. Watson.

title: A Study in Scarlet — Chapter 2 (excerpt)
content: As I hurried on, however, a hand was placed upon my arm, and I turned to find a young man beside me. His face was long, pale, and thin, and his eyes were bright and piercing. Sherlock Holmes lived at 221B Baker Street. He was a private consulting detective. He told me on our first meeting that he had methods of his own for solving problems, methods founded upon observation and deduction. Holmes was thin, over six feet, and so excessively lean that he seemed considerably taller. His eyes were sharp and piercing, save during those intervals of torpor to which I have alluded. His hands were invariably blotted with ink and stained with chemicals.

title: The Sign of the Four — Chapter 1 (excerpt)
content: Sherlock Holmes took his bottle from the corner of the mantelpiece and his hypodermic syringe from its neat morocco case. With his long, white, nervous fingers he adjusted the delicate needle, and rolled back his left shirt-cuff. Mary Morstan entered the room. She was a blonde young lady, small, dainty, well gloved, and dressed in the most perfect taste. I sat looking at her, mindful that Holmes now took his cocaine three times a day for many months. My wounded leg still ached from the campaign in Afghanistan, and I favoured it as I walked.

title: A Scandal in Bohemia — Chapter 1 (excerpt)
content: To Sherlock Holmes she is always the woman. I have seldom heard him mention her under any other name. In his eyes she eclipses and predominates the whole of her sex. Irene Adler was born in 1858 in New Jersey. She was an opera singer, a contralto, formerly of Warsaw Imperial Opera. She had married Godfrey Norton, of the Inner Temple. The King of Bohemia, a tall gentleman of noble stature, forty years of age, called at Baker Street to consult Holmes about her.
